title: W3 C Continuity in Multidim Spaces
body:
Mathematical Economics

     Juan Carlos Carbajal

  School of Economics UNSW–Sydney



  Week 3C    —    Term 1, 2026




                                    1/8
                  Existence of Solution to the UMP


 Proposition 1

   Given a continuous utility function u : Rn
                                            + → R, the consumer’s

   utility maximization problem (UMP)
                  max u(x)        subject to   p·x ≤ m
                  x≥0


   has at least one solution.


Existence of solutions to optimal behavior problems relies (in part) on the
continuity properties of functions.

Economic problems involve trade offs: buying more of good i means less
resources available for good j.
  ◦ The functions we deal with have multi-dimensional domains.




                                                                              2/8
 Continuity in Higher Dimensions




                                  3/8
                   Continuity in Higher Dimensions



Let X ⊆ Rn be an open set and f : X → R, i.e., f (x) = f (x1 , . . . , xn ).

Intuitively, f is continuous at a point x̂ ∈ X if we can get arbitrarily
close to the value f (x̂) as we get sufficiently close to x̂. Same idea as before.




                                                                                     4/8
                   Continuity in Higher Dimensions



Let X ⊆ Rn be an open set and f : X → R, i.e., f (x) = f (x1 , . . . , xn ).

Intuitively, f is continuous at a point x̂ ∈ X if we can get arbitrarily
close to the value f (x̂) as we get sufficiently close to x̂. Same idea as before.

Difficulty:
  ◦ How do we capture “getting close to” x̂ when x̂ = (x̂1 , . . . , x̂n )?

  ◦ Not so difficult after all: use the Euclidean distance in Rn .




                                                                                     4/8
                Continuity in Higher Dimensions


Definition 1

  (a) f is continuous at x̂ ∈ X if for every ϵ > 0 there exists some
      δ > 0 such that
         x ∈ X and ∥x − x̂∥ < δ        =⇒      |f (x) − f (x̂)| < ϵ.

  (b) f is continuous if it is continuous at every vector in X.




                                                                       5/8
                Continuity in Higher Dimensions


Definition 1

  (a) f is continuous at x̂ ∈ X if for every ϵ > 0 there exists some
      δ > 0 such that
         x ∈ X and ∥x − x̂∥ < δ        =⇒      |f (x) − f (x̂)| < ϵ.

  (b) f is continuous if it is continuous at every vector in X.



Example 1

 The function g(x) = a · x is continuous on X = Rn .




                                                                       5/8
                                 Example 1


If a = 0, then g(x) = a · x = 0 everywhere. So assume a ̸= 0.
To show that g is continuous at x̂ ∈ Rn , fix ϵ > 0.




                                                                6/8
                                 Example 1


If a = 0, then g(x) = a · x = 0 everywhere. So assume a ̸= 0.
To show that g is continuous at x̂ ∈ Rn , fix ϵ > 0.
                                ϵ
  ◦ Let δ ∈ R be such that δ = ∥a∥ > 0.




                                                                6/8
                                 Example 1


If a = 0, then g(x) = a · x = 0 everywhere. So assume a ̸= 0.
To show that g is continuous at x̂ ∈ Rn , fix ϵ > 0.
                                ϵ
  ◦ Let δ ∈ R be such that δ = ∥a∥ > 0.

  ◦ If x ∈ Rn is such that ∥x − x̂∥ < δ, we obtain

                      |g(x) − g(x̂)| = |a · x − a · x̂|
                                      = |a · (x − x̂)|




                                                                6/8
                                 Example 1


If a = 0, then g(x) = a · x = 0 everywhere. So assume a ̸= 0.
To show that g is continuous at x̂ ∈ Rn , fix ϵ > 0.
                                ϵ
  ◦ Let δ ∈ R be such that δ = ∥a∥ > 0.

  ◦ If x ∈ Rn is such that ∥x − x̂∥ < δ, we obtain

                      |g(x) − g(x̂)| = |a · x − a · x̂|
                                      = |a · (x − x̂)|
                                      ≤ ∥a∥ ∥x − x̂∥




                                                                6/8
                                 Example 1


If a = 0, then g(x) = a · x = 0 everywhere. So assume a ̸= 0.
To show that g is continuous at x̂ ∈ Rn , fix ϵ > 0.
                                ϵ
  ◦ Let δ ∈ R be such that δ = ∥a∥ > 0.

  ◦ If x ∈ Rn is such that ∥x − x̂∥ < δ, we obtain

                      |g(x) − g(x̂)| = |a · x − a · x̂|
                                      = |a · (x − x̂)|
                                      ≤ ∥a∥ ∥x − x̂∥
                                      < ∥a∥ δ = ϵ.




                                                                6/8
                    Somewhat More Complicated

Recall that X is an open subset of Rn . Consider a function f : X → Rm , i.e.,

                        f (x) = (f1 (x), . . . , fm (x))




                                                                                 7/8
                    Somewhat More Complicated

Recall that X is an open subset of Rn . Consider a function f : X → Rm , i.e.,

                          f (x) = (f1 (x), . . . , fm (x))

 Definition 2

    (a) f is continuous at x̂ ∈ X if for every ϵ > 0 there exists some
        δ > 0 such that
           x ∈ X and ∥x − x̂∥ < δ             =⇒      ∥f (x) − f (x̂)∥ < ϵ.

    (b) f is continuous if it is continuous at every point in X.




                                                                                 7/8
                    Somewhat More Complicated

Recall that X is an open subset of Rn . Consider a function f : X → Rm , i.e.,

                          f (x) = (f1 (x), . . . , fm (x))

 Definition 2

    (a) f is continuous at x̂ ∈ X if for every ϵ > 0 there exists some
        δ > 0 such that
           x ∈ X and ∥x − x̂∥ < δ             =⇒      ∥f (x) − f (x̂)∥ < ϵ.

    (b) f is continuous if it is continuous at every point in X.


 Example 2

   The function h(x) = A x (where A is an m × n matrix) is continuous.




                                                                                 7/8
                              Further Examples

Example 3

  (a) g1 defined by                   
                                       − x2        for x < 0,
                           g1 (x) =
                                      x + 1        for x ≥ 0
                                               10


      is not continuous at x = 0.

 (b) g2 (x) = a0 + a1 x + a2 x2 + a3 x3 is continuous.
                      x3        √
                         1
  (c) g3 (x1 , x2 ) = x1 +5 −    x2 is continuous on R2++ .

 (d) g4 : Rn → R defined by g4 (x) = ∥x∥ is continuous.




                                                                 8/8
                                Further Examples

 Example 3

    (a) g1 defined by                   
                                         − x2        for x < 0,
                             g1 (x) =
                                        x + 1        for x ≥ 0
                                                 10


        is not continuous at x = 0.

    (b) g2 (x) = a0 + a1 x + a2 x2 + a3 x3 is continuous.
                        x3        √
                           1
    (c) g3 (x1 , x2 ) = x1 +5 −    x2 is continuous on R2++ .

    (d) g4 : Rn → R defined by g4 (x) = ∥x∥ is continuous.


Working with the (ϵ, δ) definition of continuity is hard. It would be helpful to
find another way to show continuity.



                                                                                   8/8
